results.question: What is the difference between the RingCentral Analytics API and the Call Log API? When should I use each?
results.ground_truth: Analytics API: aggregated business metrics for managers — supports aggregate (POST /analytics/calls/v1/accounts/~/aggregation/fetch) and timeline endpoints. Groups data by Queue, Users, IVRs, Departments etc. Shows call counts, abandonment rates, handle time, agent performance. Covers up to 184 days of history. Call Log API: individual call records with full per-call detail (duration, direction, recording links). Use Analytics for team performance dashboards and trends; use Call Log when you need per-call data like recordings or timestamps.

raw.answer:
The RingCentral docs treat these as **two separate systems** for different kinds of call data. The core distinction:

> **Call Log APIs** are suitable for getting **metadata of individual calls**. **Analytics APIs** are suitable for **aggregate call analysis**.

They use **different data sources** (Call Log uses an older source), so a **100% match between the two is unlikely**, though RingCentral is working to reduce the gap.

## At a glance

| | **Call Log API** | **Analytics API (Business Analytics)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Granularity** | One record per call (with legs, participants, recordings) | Rolled-up metrics across many calls |
| **HTTP method** | `GET` | `POST` |
| **Primary endpoints** | `GET /restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/call-log` (extension or account scope) | `POST /analytics/calls/v1/accounts/~/aggregation/fetch` and `POST /analytics/calls/v1/accounts/~/timeline/fetch` |
| **What you get** | Per-call fields: direction, duration, start/end times, result, from/to, recording URLs | Counters and timers: call counts, handle time, abandonment, queue SLA, call segments (IVR, hold, transfer, etc.) |
| **Grouping** | Paginated list of individual records | Group by Users, Queues, IVRs, Departments, Sites, UserGroups, and more |
| **History** | Not stored indefinitely — meant for export/archival to your own storage | Current date back **184 days** |
| **Role** | **Authoritative record** of calls on the network | Historical **performance analytics** for RingEX managers |

## Call Log API — when to use it

Use Call Log when you need **per-call detail**:

- **CRM integrations** — log individual calls with caller, callee, timestamps, and outcome
- **Recordings** — access `recording.contentUri` and recording metadata per call
- **Billing / time tracking** — bill customers based on time spent on specific calls
- **Compliance and archival** — export call history to long-term storage (RingCentral does not keep call logs forever)
- **Specific call lookup** — fetch a single call by ID or filter by session, direction, date range

Call Log is **not** intended for real-time reporting or long-polling; for live events, use webhooks or telephony session events instead.

## Analytics API — when to use it

Use Analytics when you need **aggregated metrics and trends** for managers and dashboards:

- **Team and agent performance** — answered vs. not answered, handle time, agent activity across queues or departments
- **Operational dashboards** — call volume, abandonment rates, queue SLA (`InSla` / `OutOfSla`), business vs. after-hours split
- **Trend analysis** — the Timeline API breaks metrics by Hour, Day, Week, or Month (e.g., peak call times, daily handle-time trends)
- **Call flow analysis** — time in setup, ringing, IVR, live talk, hold, park, transfer, voicemail

The Aggregate API gives totals per group (e.g., total answered calls per agent); the Timeline API adds time-series breakdowns.

## Decision guide

| Your goal | Use |
|---|---|
| "Show me every call John made yesterday, with recording links" | **Call Log API** |
| "Build a dashboard of queue abandonment rate by team this month" | **Analytics API** |
| "Sync calls into Salesforce one record at a time" | **Call Log API** |
| "Chart average handle time trending over the last 90 days" | **Analytics API (Timeline)** |
| "Archive all company call history to our data warehouse" | **Call Log API** |
| "Compare agent performance across departments" | **Analytics API (Aggregate)** |

**Rule of thumb:** Call Log answers *"what happened on this specific call?"* Analytics answers *"how is my team performing across many calls over time?"*

mintlify.answer:
The RingCentral docs draw a clear line between these two APIs: **Call Log is for individual call records; Analytics is for aggregated performance metrics.** They are separate systems with different data sources, so counts will not always match exactly.

## Core difference

Per the [Analytics overview](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/analytics/index#how-is-business-analytics-api-different-than-call-log-api):

> **Call Log APIs** are suitable for getting **metadata of individual calls**.  
> **Analytics APIs** are suitable for **aggregate call analysis**.

The docs also warn that the two APIs use **different data sources** (Call Log uses an older source), so a **100% match is unlikely**, though RingCentral is working to reduce the gap.

| | **Call Log API** | **Business Analytics API** |
|---|---|---|
| **Granularity** | One record per call (optionally per leg) | Summarized metrics across many calls |
| **Data model** | Authoritative call history | Pre-aggregated counters & timers |
| **Best for** | Per-call detail, recordings, CRM sync, billing | Dashboards, KPIs, team/queue performance |
| **API style** | `GET` (REST Voice API) | `POST` (Analytics API) |
| **History window** | Not indefinite — RingCentral does not store call log forever | Up to **184 days** back |

---

## Call Log API — when to use it

Use the **Call Log API** (part of the [Voice API](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/voice/index)) when you need **individual call metadata** or **recordings**.

**Endpoint (production):**

```http
GET https://platform.ringcentral.com/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/call-log
```

**What you get per call:**
- Participants (`from`, `to`)
- `startTime`, `duration`
- `direction` (`Inbound` / `Outbound`)
- `result` (e.g. `Accepted`, `Missed`, `Voicemail`)
- Per-leg routing detail with `view=Detailed`
- Recording metadata and a `contentUri` to download audio

**Documented use cases:**
- **Archive/export** call history to your own storage (RingCentral does not keep it indefinitely)
- **CRM integration** — log specific calls to Salesforce, HubSpot, etc.
- **Billing/time tracking** — bill customers by actual call duration
- **Compliance/audit** — retain a per-call record of who called whom and when

**Required app permissions:**
- `ReadCallLog` — call log records
- `ReadCallRecording` — recording metadata and content

**Important limitations:**
- Labeled a **"Heavy"** usage plan — avoid high-frequency polling
- **Not for real-time reporting** — completed calls appear after **15–30 seconds**
- **Not for long-polling** — use [Webhooks](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/notifications/webhooks/creating-webhooks) or [Telephony Sessions events](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/voice/call-log/recordings#subscribe-to-telephony-session-events) instead (`/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/telephony/sessions`)

---

## Business Analytics API — when to use it

Use the **RingCentral Business Analytics APIs** when you need **rolled-up metrics** for reporting, not individual call rows. This is a **RingEX** feature aimed at managers tracking team, queue, and call-handling performance.

**Two report types:**

1. **Aggregate API** — sums, averages, min/max, percentages  
   `POST /analytics/calls/v1/accounts/~/aggregation/fetch`

2. **Timeline API** — same metrics split by time interval (`Hour`, `Day`, `Week`, `Month`)  
   `POST /analytics/calls/v1/accounts/~/timeline/fetch?interval=Week&page=2&perPage=10`

**Example request headers:**

```http
Host: platform.ringcentral.com
Content-Type: application/json
Accept: application/json
Authorization: Bearer REPLACE_WITH_YOUR_VALID_ACCESS_TOKEN
```

**Metrics you can analyze:**
- Call origin (internal vs external), direction, answered vs not answered
- Time in call phases: Setup, Ringing, IVR, Live Talk, Hold, etc.
- Hold/park/transfer durations
- How calls ended (voicemail, transfer, etc.)
- Business hours vs after-hours

**Grouping options include:** `Company`, `Users`, `Queues`, `IVRs`, `Sites`, `Departments`, `UserGroups`, and more.

**Example use cases from the docs:**
- "How many calls were answered this week?"
- "What is the abandonment rate per agent?"
- "What is average handle time, hourly or daily?"
- "When are peak call times?" (staffing decisions)

**Pagination limits:**
- Aggregate: max **200** results per page
- Timeline: max **20** results per page

---

## Decision guide

```
Need a specific call's details, recording, or CRM log entry?
  → Call Log API

Need team/queue KPIs, trends, or averages over days/weeks?
  → Analytics API

Need real-time call events?
  → Neither — use Webhooks / Telephony Sessions events

Need to store call history long-term?
  → Call Log API (export periodically)

Need "calls answered vs missed per agent this month"?
  → Analytics API (don't pull every call and aggregate yourself)
```

The Call Log docs themselves recommend Analytics when you want **aggregated data about an organization's call history**, rather than downloading and computing it from raw call records.

---

## SDK / packages

Neither API has a dedicated npm or pip package. Both are accessed via the standard RingCentral SDK:

```bash
npm install @ringcentral/sdk dotenv --save
```

Production base URL for Call Log: `https://platform.ringcentral.com/restapi/v1.0/`  
Sandbox: `https://platform.devtest.ringcentral.com/restapi/v1.0/`

---

## Practical takeaway

- **Call Log** = "What happened on *this* call?" (participants, duration, recording, routing legs)
- **Analytics** = "How is the team performing over time?" (answer rates, handle time, abandonment, volume trends)

If you need both — e.g. a dashboard plus drill-down into a specific call — use **Analytics for the summary view** and **Call Log for the detail view**, and expect the numbers may not match exactly between the two.

scores.raw_reasoning: Correctly distinguishes aggregated Analytics (aggregate/timeline endpoints, groupBy Queues/Users/IVRs/Departments, 184-day window, KPIs) from per-call Call Log detail (recordings, timestamps) and gives clear usage guidance with no significant errors.
scores.mintlify_reasoning: Accurately and completely covers all key facts—Analytics aggregation/timeline, grouping dimensions, 184-day history, per-call Call Log fields including recordings, and when to use each—with only helpful extra detail and no material mistakes.

results.question: How do I create a RingCentral Webinar using the API? What key settings are available, and how do I add registrants for a marketing webinar?
results.ground_truth: Create a webinar via the REST API specifying settings including: registrationEnabled (true for marketing webinars — gives each attendee a unique join URL), recordingEnabled, autoRecord, qnaEnabled, pollsEnabled, password, attendeeAuthentication. After creating the webinar, create a session. To add registrants for a marketing webinar, call the create registrant endpoint. Requires EditWebinars and ReadWebinars permissions. Set registrationEnabled=false for internal all-hands webinars (single shared join URL).

raw.answer:
Here is how to create a marketing webinar and add registrants using the RingCentral Webinar API.

## Prerequisites

Create a REST API app in the [Developer Console](https://developers.ringcentral.com/) with both permissions:

- **`EditWebinars`** — create/update webinars, sessions, and registrants  
- **`ReadWebinars`** — read webinar data  

Without both, Webinar API calls return `HTTP 403 Forbidden`.

## Workflow: webinar → session → registrants

A **webinar** is the top-level container (title, description, settings). A **session** is a scheduled occurrence with a date/time — hosts, panelists, and registrants are attached to the session, not the webinar itself.

### 1. Create the webinar

```http
POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "title": "Product Launch Q3 2026",
  "description": "Quarterly product update webinar",
  "settings": {
    "registrationEnabled": true,
    "recordingEnabled": true,
    "autoRecord": false,
    "attendeeAuthentication": "Guest",
    "password": "s3cur3pass",
    "qnaEnabled": true,
    "pollsEnabled": true,
    "postWebinarRedirectUri": "https://acme.com/thank-you"
  }
}
```

For a **marketing webinar**, set **`registrationEnabled: true`**. That gives each attendee a unique join URL for tracking and analytics. For internal all-hands meetings, set it to `false` (single shared join URL).

### 2. Schedule a session

```http
POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars/{webinarId}/sessions
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "scheduledStartTime": "2026-07-15T14:00:00Z",
  "scheduledDuration": 3600,
  "timeZone": "America/New_York",
  "localeCode": "en-US"
}
```

Required session fields: `scheduledStartTime`, `scheduledDuration` (30–43,200 seconds), `timeZone`, and `localeCode`.

### 3. (Optional) Invite hosts, co-hosts, and panelists

```http
POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars/{webinarId}/sessions/{sessionId}/invitees
```

Specify the invitee role as `Host`, `CoHost`, or `Panelist`.

---

## Key webinar settings

| Setting | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `registrationEnabled` | **`true`** for marketing webinars; **`false`** for internal all-hands |
| `recordingEnabled` | Enables recording (required for other recording settings) |
| `autoRecord` | Auto-start recording when the webinar goes live |
| `panelistWaitingRoom` | Place panelists in a waiting room on join |
| `attendeeAuthentication` | `Guest`, `AuthenticatedUser`, or `AuthenticatedCoworker` |
| `password` | Attendee password to join |
| `qnaEnabled` / `pollsEnabled` | Enable Q&A and polls |
| `postWebinarRedirectUri` | URL to redirect attendees after the webinar ends |

Additional settings in the SDK/API include `panelistVideoEnabled`, `panelistScreenSharingEnabled`, `qnaAnonymousEnabled`, `moderatedQnaEnabled`, `pstnEnabled`, and `externalLivestreamEnabled`. See the full list in the [Webinar API Reference](https://developers.ringcentral.com/api-reference/webinar).

---

## Adding registrants (marketing webinars)

Call the [create registrant](https://developers.ringcentral.com/api-reference/Registrants/rcwRegCreateRegistrant) operation:

```http
POST /webinar/registration/v1/sessions/{sessionId}/registrants
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "firstName": "Jane",
  "lastName": "Smith",
  "email": "jane@example.com",
  "externalId": "lid=123&oid=456",
  "source": "customer-invite"
}
```

**Required fields:** `firstName`, `lastName`, `email`.

**Useful optional fields for marketing integrations:**

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `externalId` | Link to a CRM contact/lead/opportunity (can encode multiple IDs as a query string) |
| `source` | Campaign attribution (e.g. `"customer-invite"`, `"search-ad"`) |
| `joinUri` / `cancellationUri` | Custom join/cancel pages on your site instead of RingCentral-hosted pages |
| `questionnaire` | Answers to custom registration questions |

You can register people in advance or just-in-time (e.g. when someone clicks "Join" on your site). Every attendee must register before joining.

### Registration preferences (per session)

Update session-level registration settings via `PATCH /webinar/registration/v1/sessions/{sessionId}`:

- `registrationStatus` — open/close registration  
- `autoCloseLimit` — auto-close after N registrants  
- `suppressEmails` — stop RingCentral from sending registration emails (you handle them)  
- `preventMultipleDeviceJoins` — block multi-device joins  
- `registrantCount` / `hasRealRegistrants` — read-only registration stats  

### Tracking attendance

Each registrant gets a unique `id`. When they join, they receive a `participantId`. If both are present, they attended; if `participantId` is missing, they never joined. You can also subscribe to registrant webhooks at `/webinar/registration/v1/company/sessions/registrants`.

---

## SDK note

The Node.js, Python, PHP, and Ruby SDKs support Webinar APIs. Java and C# SDKs do not currently — call the REST endpoints directly from those languages.

**Docs in this repo:** `docs/webinar/creation.mdx`, `docs/webinar/registrants.mdx`, and `docs/webinar/quick-start.mdx`.

mintlify.answer:
Creating a RingCentral marketing webinar via the API is a three-step flow: create the webinar container, schedule a session, then register attendees. Here is how it works, based on the [Webinar creation](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/webinar/creation), [Quick Start](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/webinar/quick-start), and [Registrants](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/webinar/registrants) guides.

## Prerequisites

1. **Create a REST API app** in the [Developer Console](https://developers.ringcentral.com/) with **JWT auth flow**.
2. **Grant both permissions**: `EditWebinars` and `ReadWebinars`. Without them, all Webinar API calls return **`HTTP 403 Forbidden`**.
3. **Purchase a Webinar license** via the Admin Portal (the Webinar API is currently in **beta**).
4. **Authenticate** with a JWT and use the RingCentral SDK or direct REST calls against `https://platform.ringcentral.com`.

**SDK install commands** (from the Quick Start):

```bash
npm install @ringcentral/sdk dotenv          # Node.js
pip3 install ringcentral                     # Python
composer require ringcentral/ringcentral-php # PHP
gem install ringcentral-sdk                  # Ruby
```

Set these environment variables: `RC_SERVER_URL`, `RC_APP_CLIENT_ID`, `RC_APP_CLIENT_SECRET`, `RC_USER_JWT`.

> **Note:** Java and C# SDKs do not currently support Webinar APIs; call the REST endpoints directly from those languages.

---

## Step 1: Create the webinar

**Endpoint:** `POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars`  
**API Reference:** [rcwConfCreateWebinar](https://developers.ringcentral.com/api-reference/webinar)

Required body fields: `title` and optionally `description`. Pass behavior settings inside a `settings` object.

```javascript
platform.post('/webinar/configuration/v1/webinars', {
  title: "Q3 Product Launch Webinar",
  description: "Marketing webinar for external prospects",
  settings: {
    registrationEnabled: true,   // required for marketing webinars
    recordingEnabled: true,
    autoRecord: true,
    qnaEnabled: true,
    pollsEnabled: true,
    password: "optional-password",
    postWebinarRedirectUri: "https://acme.com/thank-you"
  }
})
```

Save the returned **`webinarId`** from the response.

---

## Step 2: Schedule a webinar session

A **webinar** is a settings container; a **session** is the dated event attendees register for and join.

**Endpoint:** `POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars/{webinarId}/sessions`  
**API Reference:** [rcwConfCreateSession](https://developers.ringcentral.com/api-reference/webinar)

Required session fields:

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| `scheduledStartTime` | ISO 8601 datetime, e.g. `"2026-07-22T09:00:00.000Z"` |
| `scheduledDuration` | Duration in **seconds**, e.g. `3600` for 1 hour |
| `timezone` | IANA timezone, e.g. `"America/New_York"` |

```javascript
platform.post('/webinar/configuration/v1/webinars/' + webinarId + '/sessions', {
  scheduledStartTime: "2026-07-22T09:00:00.000Z",
  scheduledDuration: 3600,
  timezone: "America/New_York"
})
```

Save the returned **`sessionId`**. Webinars are currently limited to **one session** per webinar.

---

## Key webinar settings

These go in the `settings` object when creating (or updating) the webinar. The full list is in the [Webinar API Reference](https://developers.ringcentral.com/api-reference/webinar).

| Setting | Description |
|---|---|
| `registrationEnabled` | **`true` for marketing webinars** — each attendee gets a unique join URL. Set `false` for internal all-hands meetings. |
| `recordingEnabled` | Enables recording; if `false`, other recording settings are ignored. |
| `autoRecord` | Starts recording automatically when the webinar goes live. |
| `panelistWaitingRoom` | Places panelists in a waiting room after joining. |
| `attendeeAuthentication` | Values: `Guest`, `AuthenticatedUser`, `AuthenticatedCoworker`. |
| `password` | Optional attendee password. |
| `qnaEnabled` | Enables/disables Q&A. |
| `pollsEnabled` | Enables/disables polls. |
| `postWebinarRedirectUri` | URL to redirect attendees after the webinar ends. |

### Registration session preferences

After the session exists, manage registration behavior via the [rcwRegUpdateSession](https://developers.ringcentral.com/api-reference/Registration-Management/rcwRegUpdateSession) operation:

| Preference | Description |
|---|---|
| `registrationStatus` | Turn registration on/off for the session. |
| `registrantCount` | Read-only count of registered attendees. |
| `autoCloseLimit` | Close registration after N registrants. |
| `suppressEmails` | Suppress RingCentral registration emails (useful if you send your own). |
| `preventMultipleDeviceJoins` | Block joining from multiple devices. |

---

## Step 3: Add registrants (marketing webinar)

For **marketing webinars** (external attendees), use the registrant API. Registration endpoints are **not intended for internal all-hands meetings** — those should use `registrationEnabled: false` and a shared join link instead.

**Endpoint:** `POST /webinar/registration/v1/sessions/{sessionId}/registrants`  
**API Reference:** [rcwRegCreateRegistrant](https://developers.ringcentral.com/api-reference/Registrants/rcwRegCreateRegistrant)

Registration must be **open** for the session; otherwise the call returns **`HTTP 403`**.

```javascript
platform.post('/webinar/registration/v1/sessions/' + sessionId + '/registrants', {
  firstName: 'Luke',
  lastName: 'Skywalker',
  email: 'luke.skywalker@jedi.org',
  externalId: 'crm-lead-123',           // optional: CRM correlation
  source: 'website-campaign-123',       // optional: campaign tracking
  joinUri: 'https://acme.com/webinar/join?id=5678',       // optional: custom join page
  cancellationUri: 'https://acme.com/webinar/cancel?id=5678'  // optional: custom cancel page
})
```

### Typical marketing integration pattern

1. Build a custom sign-up page on your site.
2. On form submit, call `rcwRegCreateRegistrant` from your backend.
3. Set `suppressEmails: true` if you send confirmation/reminder emails yourself.
4. Use `source` to tag the campaign (e.g. `"email-invite"` vs `"search-ad"`).
5. Use `externalId` to link the registrant to a CRM lead/contact ID.
6. Each registrant receives a unique `joinUri` in the response for tracking attendance.

### Tracking attendance

- Each registrant gets a unique `id`.
- When they join, they also get a `participantId`.
- If a registrant has both `id` and `participantId`, they attended; if `participantId` is missing, they did not join.
- Subscribe to webhook event filter `/webinar/registration/v1/company/sessions/registrants` (optionally scoped with `?webinarId={webinarId}`) for real-time registration updates.

---

## End-to-end flow summary

```
1. POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars
   → settings.registrationEnabled = true
```

results.question: What types of events can I subscribe to using the RingCentral push notification service? Name at least 5 distinct event categories.
results.ground_truth: RingCentral subscriptions support: (1) Telephony/call events — active call sessions and state changes; (2) Message events — incoming SMS, voicemail, fax via /message-store; (3) Presence events — user presence/availability changes; (4) Extension/account events — extension updates; (5) Call queue events — queue member activity; (6) AI Conversation Expert events — ACE analysis complete; (7) Meeting/video events. All subscriptions are created via POST /restapi/v1.0/subscription with an eventFilters array. WebSocket or webhook delivery.

raw.answer:
RingCentral’s push notification service is the **Subscriptions API**. You create subscriptions with `POST /restapi/v1.0/subscription`, list the events you want in an `eventFilters` array, and choose delivery via **webhook** (server-side) or **WebSocket** (client-side, e.g. mobile via `@ringcentral/subscriptions`).

Per the monorepo docs, here are **7 distinct event categories** you can subscribe to:

### 1. **Message events**
Notifications when new messages arrive in the message store, including:
- **SMS received**
- **Voicemail received**
- **Fax received**

Example filter: `/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/message-store/instant?type=SMS`

### 2. **Telephony / call events**
Real-time call activity, such as:
- **Call started / call ended**
- **Missed call**
- **Telephony session state changes** (account- or extension-scoped via `/telephony/sessions`)

These are the recommended alternative to polling the call log for real-time use cases.

### 3. **Presence events**
Changes to a user’s **presence/availability** (e.g., Available, Do Not Disturb, on a call, in a meeting).

Example filter: `/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/presence`

### 4. **Team Messaging (Glip) events**
Events from RingCentral Team Messaging, such as:
- **New posts/messages** (`/restapi/v1.0/glip/posts`)
- **Group/team membership changes** (`/restapi/v1.0/glip/groups`)

### 5. **AI Conversation Expert (ACE) events**
Notifications when **ACE finishes analyzing** a recorded voice call or video meeting. The event payload includes ACE insight data.

### 6. **Webinar events**
Webinar-specific push events (via the Webinar Subscriptions API), including:
- Session created/modified
- Session state changes (start/end)
- Registration setting changes
- Registrant created/modified

### 7. **Subscription lifecycle events**
Meta-events about your subscription itself, such as **expiration reminders** so you can renew before it lapses.

Example filter: `/restapi/v1.0/subscription/~?threshold=3600&interval=3600`

---

The [Events and Notifications overview](docs/notifications/index.mdx) lists SMS, voicemail, fax, call start/end, missed call, and presence as supported events, and points to the [API Reference](https://developers.ringcentral.com/api-reference/Account-Presence-Event) for the full catalog of event filters.

mintlify.answer:
The RingCentral push notification service lets you subscribe to platform events via **webhooks** (server-side) or **WebSockets** (client/mobile). Subscriptions are created with the Subscription API:

```http
POST /restapi/v1.0/subscription
```

You specify which events you want in the `eventFilters` array. For mobile/WebSocket clients, RingCentral recommends the `@ringcentral/subscriptions` package:

```bash
npm install @ringcentral/subscriptions --save
```

Per the [Events and Notifications documentation](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/notifications/index), here are the main **event categories** you can subscribe to (well more than five):

---

### 1. Message Store / Messaging events

Notifications when messages are received or sent in the Message Store, including:

- **SMS/MMS received** — instant inbound text messages  
  Event filter: `/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/message-store/instant?type=SMS`
- **Voicemail received**
- **Fax received**
- **Pager messages**

Message types supported in the Message Store include `SMS`, `VoiceMail`, `Fax`, and `Pager`. Fax and other message types use the same message-store push notification pattern (see the [Receiving Faxes](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/messaging/fax/receiving-faxes) guide).

---

### 2. Telephony / Voice call events

Real-time call lifecycle notifications:

- **Call started and call ended**
- **Missed call** — fired when a call is never answered or picked up
- **Account Telephony Sessions** — triggered whenever the state of a call changes (account-wide)
- **Extension Telephony Sessions** — same as above, scoped to a specific user/extension

Example event filter for extension telephony sessions:

`/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/telephony/sessions`

These are the recommended approach for real-time call tracking instead of polling the Call Log API.

---

### 3. Presence / Availability events

Notifications when a user's availability changes across the platform — on the phone, in a meeting, Do Not Disturb, etc. Fields include `presenceStatus`, `telephonyStatus`, `userStatus`, and `dndStatus`.

The docs point to the [Account Presence Event API Reference](https://developers.ringcentral.com/api-reference/Account-Presence-Event) for the full list of presence event filters. Presence subscriptions require the `ReadPresence` permission; related error codes include `SUB-402` through `SUB-404`.

---

### 4. Team Messaging events

For bots and Team Messaging integrations, you can subscribe to events such as:

- **Posts** (messages sent to a bot)
- **Groups** (team/group membership changes)
- Bot lifecycle events (bot extension created, personal chat created, bot added to a group)

Bot apps subscribe after installation using the same push notification framework. The bot walkthrough shows subscribing to "posts and groups events."

---

### 5. Webinar events

Webinar has its own event filters (via a separate subscription API). Available filters include:

| Event Filter | Description |
|---|---|
| `/webinar/configuration/v1/company/sessions` | Session created or modified |
| `/webinar/runtime/v1/company/sessions/state` | Session state changes (start/end) |
| `/webinar/registration/v1/company/sessions/state` | Registration settings modified |
| `/webinar/registration/v1/company/sessions/registrants` | Registrant created or modified |

These can be narrowed with `?webinarId=` or `?hostUserId=` query parameters.

---

### 6. AI Conversation Expert (ACE) events

Notifications when AI Conversation Expert finishes conversational analysis of a recorded voice call or video meeting. The event payload includes ACE insights (transcript, summary, sentiment, etc.). These require an active ACE license on the recording user's extension.

---

### 7. Subscription management events

Meta-events about your subscriptions themselves — for example, reminders before a webhook subscription expires:

`/restapi/v1.0/subscription/~?threshold=XXX&interval=YYY`

- `threshold` — seconds remaining before expiration when reminders start  
- `interval` — seconds between reminder notifications

---

### 8. APNS (Apple Push Notification Service) events

RingCentral also supports **APNS subscriptions** for native iOS push delivery. These require an `endpoint_id` to be defined (error `SUB-407` if missing).

---

### Summary table

| Category | Example events |
|---|---|
| **Messaging** | SMS received, voicemail received, fax received |
| **Telephony** | Call started/ended, missed call, telephony session state changes |
| **Presence** | Availability/status changed |
| **Team Messaging** | Posts, groups, bot lifecycle |
| **Webinar** | Session config, state, registration, registrants |
| **AI (ACE)** | Conversation analysis completed |
| **Subscription meta** | Expiration/renewal reminders |
| **APNS** | Native iOS push notifications |

For the complete list of event filter paths and parameters, the docs refer to the [Event Filters index](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/notifications/event-filters/index) and the [Subscription API Reference](https://developers.ringcentral.com/api-reference/Subscriptions/createSubscription). Webhook subscriptions also require your server to echo back the `Validation-Token` HTTP header during URL validation.

results.question: Show me how to send an SMS using the RingCentral REST API in both JavaScript/Node.js and Ruby. What is the endpoint and key request fields?
results.ground_truth: Endpoint: POST /restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/sms. Required fields: from.phoneNumber, to[].phoneNumber, text. JavaScript: platform.post('/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/sms', {from: {phoneNumber: '...'}, to: [{phoneNumber: '...'}], text: 'Hello'}) using @ringcentral/sdk. Ruby: platform.post('/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/sms', payload: {from: {phoneNumber: '...'}, to: [{phoneNumber: '...'}], text: 'Hello'}) using ringcentral_sdk gem.

raw.answer:
Here is how to send an SMS with the RingCentral REST API, based on the docs and SDK examples in this monorepo.

## Endpoint

| Item | Value |
|------|-------|
| **Method** | `POST` |
| **Path** | `/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/sms` |
| **Production base URL** | `https://platform.ringcentral.com` |
| **Sandbox base URL** | `https://platform.devtest.ringcentral.com` |

The `~` placeholders resolve to the authenticated account and extension. Full production URL:

```
POST https://platform.ringcentral.com/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/sms
```

## Key request fields

Send a JSON body with these fields:

| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|-------|------|----------|-------------|
| `from.phoneNumber` | string | Yes | Sender number in E.164 format (e.g. `+15551234567`). Must be assigned to the authenticated extension and SMS-enabled (`SmsSender` feature). |
| `to[].phoneNumber` | array of objects | Yes | One or more recipients, each with a `phoneNumber` in E.164 format. |
| `text` | string | Yes | Message body (max 1000 UTF-16 symbols). |

Example JSON body:

```json
{
  "from": { "phoneNumber": "+15551234567" },
  "to": [{ "phoneNumber": "+15559876543" }],
  "text": "Hello from RingCentral!"
}
```

**Prerequisites:** Your app needs the **SMS** permission, JWT (or OAuth) authentication, and the sender number must be SMS-capable. On production accounts, TCR registration is also required before sending SMS.

---

## JavaScript / Node.js

Install the SDK:

```bash
npm install @ringcentral/sdk dotenv --save
```

Example (`sms.js`):

```javascript
require('dotenv').config()
const { SDK } = require('@ringcentral/sdk')

const rcsdk = new SDK({
    server: process.env.RC_SERVER_URL || 'https://platform.ringcentral.com',
    clientId: process.env.RC_APP_CLIENT_ID,
    clientSecret: process.env.RC_APP_CLIENT_SECRET
})

async function sendSms() {
    const platform = rcsdk.platform()
    await platform.login({ jwt: process.env.RC_USER_JWT })

    const response = await platform.post('/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/sms', {
        from: { phoneNumber: '+15551234567' },
        to: [{ phoneNumber: '+15559876543' }],
        text: 'Hello from RingCentral!'
    })

    console.log(await response.json())
}

sendSms()
```

The SDK wraps the same REST call shown in `sdks/ringcentral-js/sdk/README.md` — `platform.post()` to the SMS endpoint with `from`, `to`, and `text`.

---

## Ruby

Install the official SDK:

```bash
gem install ringcentral-sdk dotenv
```

Example (`sms.rb`):

```ruby
require 'ringcentral'
require 'dotenv/load'

rc = RingCentral.new(
  ENV['RC_APP_CLIENT_ID'],
  ENV['RC_APP_CLIENT_SECRET'],
  ENV['RC_SERVER_URL'] || 'https://platform.ringcentral.com'
)
rc.authorize(jwt: ENV['RC_USER_JWT'])

r = rc.post('/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/sms', payload: {
  from: { phoneNumber: '+15551234567' },
  to: [{ phoneNumber: '+15559876543' }],
  text: 'Hello from RingCentral!'
})

puts r.body
```

In Ruby, the JSON body is passed via the `payload:` keyword argument (see `sdks/ringcentral-ruby/README.md`).

---

## Summary

Both SDKs call the same endpoint with the same body shape:

- **Endpoint:** `POST /restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/sms`
- **Required fields:** `from.phoneNumber`, `to[].phoneNumber`, `text`
- **JS:** `platform.post('/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/sms', { from, to, text })` via `@ringcentral/sdk`
- **Ruby:** `rc.post('/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/sms', payload: { from, to, text })` via `ringcentral-sdk`

Before sending, you can list SMS-capable numbers with `GET /restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/phone-number` and confirm the number’s `features` array includes `"SmsSender"`.

mintlify.answer:
Here is how to send an SMS with the RingCentral REST API, based on the [SMS Quick Start](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/messaging/quick-start) and [Sending SMS](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/messaging/sms/sending-sms) docs.

## Endpoint

| Item | Value |
|------|-------|
| **Method** | `POST` |
| **Path** | `/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/sms` |
| **Production URL** | `https://platform.ringcentral.com/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/sms` |
| **Sandbox URL** | `https://platform.devtest.ringcentral.com/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/sms` |

The `~` shorthand means “the authenticated user’s account/extension.”

## Key request fields

### HTTP headers

| Header | Value |
|--------|-------|
| `Authorization` | `Bearer <access-token>` |
| `Content-Type` | `application/json` |
| `Accept` | `application/json` (recommended) |

### JSON body (required fields)

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `from.phoneNumber` | string | Sender number in E.164 format (e.g. `+16505550100`). Must belong to the authenticated extension and have the `SmsSender` feature. |
| `to` | array | One or more recipient objects: `{ "phoneNumber": "+1..." }` (up to 10 for group SMS). |
| `text` | string | Message body. |

Example request:

```http
POST /restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/sms HTTP/1.1
Host: platform.ringcentral.com
Authorization: Bearer <access-token>
Content-Type: application/json
Accept: application/json

{
  "from": { "phoneNumber": "+16505550100" },
  "to": [{ "phoneNumber": "+16505550101" }],
  "text": "Hello World!"
}
```

### Prerequisites (from the docs)

Before calling the SMS endpoint:

1. Authenticate a **user extension** (JWT is used in the quick-start samples).
2. Read phone numbers via `GET /restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/phone-number`.
3. Pick a number whose `features` array includes `"SmsSender"`.
4. Use that number as `from.phoneNumber`.

Your app also needs the **SMS** and **ReadAccounts** permissions.

---

## JavaScript / Node.js

**Package:** `@ringcentral/sdk`  
**Install:**

```bash
npm install @ringcentral/sdk dotenv --save
```

**Run:**

```bash
node sms.js
```

**Example (`sms.js`):**

```javascript
const RC = require('@ringcentral/sdk').SDK
require('dotenv').config()

const RECIPIENT = process.env.SMS_RECIPIENT

const rcsdk = new RC({
  server: 'https://platform.ringcentral.com',
  clientId: process.env.RC_APP_CLIENT_ID,
  clientSecret: process.env.RC_APP_CLIENT_SECRET
})
const platform = rcsdk.platform()

platform.login({ jwt: process.env.RC_USER_JWT })

platform.on(platform.events.loginSuccess, () => {
  read_extension_phone_number_detect_sms_feature()
})

async function read_extension_phone_number_detect_sms_feature() {
  const resp = await platform.get('/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/phone-number')
  const jsonObj = await resp.json()

  for (const record of jsonObj.records) {
    if (record.features.includes('SmsSender')) {
      return send_sms(record.phoneNumber)
    }
  }
}

async function send_sms(fromNumber) {
  const bodyParams = {
    from: { phoneNumber: fromNumber },
    to: [{ phoneNumber: RECIPIENT }],
    text: 'Hello World!'
  }

  const resp = await platform.post('/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/sms', bodyParams)
  const jsonObj = await resp.json()
  console.log('SMS sent. Message id:', jsonObj.id)
}
```

**Environment variables:**

- `RC_APP_CLIENT_ID`
- `RC_APP_CLIENT_SECRET`
- `RC_USER_JWT`
- `SMS_RECIPIENT`

---

## Ruby

**Package:** `ringcentral-sdk` (SDK table also lists `ringcentral_sdk`)  
**Install:**

```bash
gem install ringcentral-sdk dotenv
```

**Run:**

```bash
ruby sms.rb
```

**Example (`sms.rb`):**

```ruby
require 'ringcentral'
require 'dotenv'

Dotenv.load('./.env')

RECIPIENT = ENV['SMS_RECIPIENT']

$platform = RingCentral.new(
  ENV['RC_APP_CLIENT_ID'],
  ENV['RC_APP_CLIENT_SECRET'],
  'https://platform.ringcentral.com'
)

def read_extension_phone_number_detect_sms_feature
  resp = $platform.get('/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/phone-number')

  resp.body['records'].each do |record|
    if record['features'].include?('SmsSender')
      return send_sms(record['phoneNumber'])
    end
  end
end

def send_sms(fromNumber)
  bodyParams = {
    from: { phoneNumber: fromNumber },
    to: [{ phoneNumber: RECIPIENT }],
    text: 'Hello World!'
  }

  resp = $platform.post('/restapi/v1.0/account/~/extension/~/sms', payload: bodyParams)
  puts 'SMS sent. Message id: ' + resp.body['id'].to_s
end

$platform.authorize(jwt: ENV['RC_USER_JWT'])
read_extension_phone_number_detect_sms_feature
```

---

## Notes

- SMS can only be sent from numbers owned by the authenticated extension (direct number or digital line).
- A super admin **cannot** send SMS on behalf of another extension.
- Common SMS errors include `MSG-242` (number not SMS-enabled), `MSG-304` (number doesn’t belong to extension), and `MSG-240` (recipient not a US number).
- For sandbox testing, use `https://platform.devtest.ringcentral.com` as the server URL.

Docs references: [SMS Quick Start](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/messaging/quick-start), [Sending SMS](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/messaging/sms/sending-sms), [SDKs](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/sdks), [URIs](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/basics/uris).

results.question: What CRM platforms does RingCentral have native phone integration support for, and what does the rc-unified-crm-extension framework provide?
results.ground_truth: RingCentral has native phone integrations for: HubSpot (hubspot-embeddable-ringcentral-phone), Pipedrive (pipedrive-embeddable-ringcentral-phone), and Redtail (redtail-embeddable-ringcentral-phone). The rc-unified-crm-extension (App Connect) is a developer SDK/framework for building custom CRM adapters — it provides standardized call logging, contact lookup, and click-to-dial capabilities. Developers use it to connect any CRM to the RingCentral embeddable phone widget.

raw.answer:
## Native phone integrations

RingCentral ships **three ready-made native phone integrations** — Chrome extensions that embed the RingCentral phone widget into CRM UIs:

| CRM | Repository |
|-----|------------|
| **HubSpot** | `hubspot-embeddable-ringcentral-phone` |
| **Pipedrive** | `pipedrive-embeddable-ringcentral-phone` |
| **Redtail** | `redtail-embeddable-ringcentral-phone` |

These extensions provide click-to-call, inbound caller pop-ups, and call log/voicemail/SMS sync back to the CRM.

---

## `rc-unified-crm-extension` (App Connect)

**App Connect** (`rc-unified-crm-extension`) is a **developer SDK/framework** for building custom CRM adapters. It standardizes the integration surface so developers can connect **any CRM** to the RingCentral embeddable phone widget.

Core capabilities:

- **Call logging** — standardized sync of calls and messages to CRM records
- **Contact lookup** — find contacts by phone or name during calls (call pop)
- **Click-to-dial** — initiate calls from CRM contact pages

The framework also includes:

- A **connector architecture** (manifest + server implementation) with standardized interface functions (`findContact`, `createCallLog`, `updateCallLog`, etc.)
- Shared runtime via `@app-connect/core` (HTTP routes, auth/token refresh, server-side logging, plugins)
- Support for **SMS, fax, and voicemail** logging
- A **CLI** (`@app-connect/cli`) to scaffold new CRM connector projects
- Optional **MCP server** integration for AI assistants to interact with CRM data

RingCentral’s developer docs describe the three embeddable-phone repos above as native integrations **built on** App Connect, while App Connect itself is the extensible framework for building additional CRM connectors beyond those three.

mintlify.answer:
## Native Phone Integrations

Per the [Specialized SDKs](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/sdks#specialized-sdks) documentation, RingCentral ships **three ready-made native phone integrations** built on the App Connect framework (`rc-unified-crm-extension`):

| CRM Platform | Package Name |
|---|---|
| **HubSpot** | `hubspot-embeddable-ringcentral-phone` |
| **Pipedrive** | `pipedrive-embeddable-ringcentral-phone` |
| **Redtail** | `redtail-embeddable-ringcentral-phone` |

These are embeddable RingCentral phone widgets that run inside each CRM’s UI.

---

## Broader App Connect CRM Support

The `rc-unified-crm-extension` framework also powers **App Connect**, which supports a wider set of CRM platforms beyond those three native phone integrations. Documented examples include **Salesforce**, **HubSpot**, and **Zoho**, plus others supported by the App Connect product.

The full, current list is returned dynamically by the `getPublicConnectors` tool on the App Connect MCP server:

- **Endpoint:** `https://unified-crm-extension.labs.ringcentral.com/mcp`
- **Tool:** `getPublicConnectors` (no parameters required)

Each connector object includes fields such as `id`, `name`, `logoUrl`, `authType` (e.g. `"oauth2"`, `"apikey"`), and `status` (e.g. `"available"`, `"beta"`, `"deprecated"`).

---

## What `rc-unified-crm-extension` Provides

The **`rc-unified-crm-extension`** package (branded as **App Connect**) is a developer SDK/framework for building CRM integrations. It provides:

1. **Standardized call logging** — sync call activity to CRM records (via tools like `createCallLog` and `rcGetCallLogs`)
2. **Contact lookup** — search CRM contacts by name or phone number (via `findContactByName`, `findContactByPhone`)
3. **Click-to-dial** — initiate calls from within CRM contexts

**Documentation:** https://ringcentral.github.io/rc-unified-crm-extension/

### App Connect MCP Server

The same framework powers an MCP server that bridges RingCentral telephony with a connected CRM. It exposes **9 tools**, including:

| Tool | CRM Required | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `getSessionInfo` | No | Current user identity and CRM connection status |
| `getPublicConnectors` | No | List available CRM connectors |
| `getHelp` | No | Quick integration guide |
| `findContactByName` | Yes | Search CRM contacts by name |
| `findContactByPhone` | Yes | Search CRM contacts by phone number |
| `createContact` | Yes | Create a new CRM contact |
| `createCallLog` | Yes | Log a call activity to the CRM |
| `rcGetCallLogs` | Yes | Retrieve call logs from RingCentral |
| `logout` | No | Sign out from the CRM session |

**Authentication model:** Two layers — RingCentral identity (OAuth 2.0 / SSO) and CRM connection (OAuth via the App Connect portal). Tools marked as requiring CRM connection return an authentication error if no CRM is linked.

---

**Summary:** RingCentral has **three native phone integrations** (HubSpot, Pipedrive, Redtail) built on `rc-unified-crm-extension`. The framework itself is a broader CRM integration SDK providing standardized **call logging**, **contact lookup**, and **click-to-dial**, and it also supports additional CRMs (Salesforce, HubSpot, Zoho, and others) through the App Connect MCP server.

results.question: How long does a RingCentral OAuth access token last, and how do I get a new one without forcing the user to log in again? What happens when the refresh token also expires?
results.ground_truth: Access tokens last approximately 3600 seconds (1 hour). Use the refresh_token grant type (grant_type=refresh_token) to get a new access token silently before or after expiry — no user interaction needed. Refresh tokens for Auth Code flow last 7 days. If the refresh token expires, the user must re-authenticate from scratch (full OAuth flow). For JWT (server-to-server) flows there is no refresh token — simply request a new access token using the JWT grant at any time. Always cache and reuse tokens; calling the token endpoint too frequently triggers the Auth rate limit (5 requests/user/minute).

raw.answer:
## Access token lifetime

RingCentral OAuth **access tokens last about 1 hour** (~3600 seconds). The token response includes an `expires_in` field — store that with the issue timestamp and treat it as the source of truth for when to refresh.

| Token | Time to live |
|-------|--------------|
| Access token | **1 hour** |
| Refresh token | **7 days** |

## Getting a new access token without re-login

For **Authorization Code** (or **Auth Code + PKCE**) apps, use the **refresh token flow**. No user interaction is required.

1. **Enable refresh tokens** in your app’s Developer Console settings (“Issue refresh tokens”). They are **not issued by default**.

2. **Store both tokens** after the initial authorization, along with issue time and the `expires_in` / `refresh_token_expires_in` values from the response.

3. **Refresh proactively** before the access token expires (don’t wait for HTTP 401):

```http
POST /restapi/oauth/token HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Authorization: Basic {base64(client_id:client_secret)}

grant_type=refresh_token&refresh_token={your_refresh_token}
```

For **client-side web apps**, omit the `Authorization` header and pass `client_id` in the body instead.

4. **Cache and reuse** access tokens for their full 1-hour lifetime. The token endpoint is rate-limited to **5 requests per user per minute** (Auth rate limit).

5. For long-lived background access, run a **scheduled job at least once per day** to refresh stored tokens so the 7-day refresh window doesn’t lapse.

Important refresh-token behavior:
- Each refresh token is **single-use**; a new refresh token is usually returned with the new access token.
- The old access token is invalidated immediately on refresh.
- Refresh before expiry, not after a 401 — that doubles your token requests.

## When the refresh token also expires

If the **refresh token expires (after 7 days)** or is invalidated (user revokes access, password change, admin session timeout, etc.), **silent refresh is no longer possible**. The user must go through the **full OAuth authorization flow again** — there is no way to extend an expired refresh token without user interaction.

## JWT / server-to-server alternative

If your app has **no user UI** and acts as a single service account, the **JWT auth flow** is the better fit. JWT flows **do not use refresh tokens** — you simply request a new access token with the JWT grant whenever needed, with no user login:

```http
POST /restapi/oauth/token
grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer&assertion={JWT}
```

Access tokens from JWT still expire after ~1 hour, but you can mint new ones programmatically at any time using the JWT credential.

mintlify.answer:
## Access token lifetime

For OAuth **Authorization Code** (and **Auth Code + PKCE**) flows, RingCentral access tokens last about **1 hour** (~**3600 seconds**). The token response includes `expires_in` (seconds until the access token expires) and `refresh_token_expires_in` (seconds until the refresh token expires).

| Token | Time to live |
|-------|--------------|
| Access token | **1 hour** |
| Refresh token | **7 days** |

Cache and reuse the access token for its full lifetime. Do not fetch a new token on every API call — the token endpoint is limited to **5 requests per user per minute**.

---

## Getting a new access token without user login

Use the **refresh token flow**. This only works if your app has **"Issue refresh tokens"** enabled in the Developer Console (off by default).

### 1. Refresh the access token

**Endpoint:** `POST https://platform.ringcentral.com/restapi/oauth/token`

**Headers:**

| Header | Value |
|--------|-------|
| `Content-Type` | `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` |
| `Authorization` | `Basic ` + base64(`Client ID:Client Secret`) — **not** required for "Client-side web app" apps |

**Body parameters:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| `grant_type` | `refresh_token` |
| `refresh_token` | The stored refresh token |
| `client_id` | Required only for "Client-side web app" apps |

**Example request:**

```http
POST /restapi/oauth/token HTTP/1.1
Accept: application/json
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

refresh_token=BCMDFUMDRKV1MwMXx5d5dwzLFL4ec6U1A0XMsUv935527jghj48
   &grant_type=refresh_token&client_id=adsadsadsadadsad
```

The response returns a new `access_token`, a new `refresh_token`, and updated `expires_in` / `refresh_token_expires_in`. Store all of these with the issue timestamp.

### 2. Best practices

- **Refresh proactively** — before the access token expires, using stored `expires_in` and issue time. Avoid waiting for `HTTP 401`.
- **Refresh tokens are single-use** — each refresh usually issues a new refresh token. Save the new one immediately.
- **Run a background job at least once per day** — iterate stored tokens and refresh them so users stay signed in without re-logging in.
- **Use one thread for auth** — perform OAuth/refresh in a single thread and share tokens across workers to avoid race conditions.

### 3. SDK option

Official SDKs handle token management. For Node.js:

```bash
npm install @ringcentral/sdk
```

Other SDK packages: `ringcentral` (Python), `ringcentral/ringcentral-php`, `ringcentral_sdk` (Ruby), `com.ringcentral:ringcentral` (Java), `github.com/ringcentral/ringcentral-go`, `RingCentral.Net` (.NET).

For long-running processes, the docs recommend calling `platform.refresh()` periodically (e.g., daily or every 6 days) so the refresh token does not expire.

---

## When the refresh token also expires

If the refresh token reaches its **7-day** lifetime (or is otherwise invalid), **silent refresh no longer works**. The user must **re-authenticate from scratch** through the full OAuth Authorization Code (or PKCE) flow:

1. Redirect to `https://platform.ringcentral.com/restapi/oauth/authorize?...`
2. User logs in and grants consent again
3. Exchange the new authorization code for fresh tokens

There is no way to extend an expired refresh token without user interaction in the Auth Code flow.

**What you’ll see:**

- Expired **access token** on API calls: **`HTTP 401`** with error code **`OAU-128`** — `"Access token expired."`
- Expired or reused **refresh token** on the token endpoint: the refresh request fails (typically **`HTTP 400`** with a standard OAuth error such as `invalid_grant`), and you must send the user through login again

Other events that invalidate both tokens and force re-login:

- User revokes app access
- User changes password
- Admin “Absolute Session Timeout” policy
- App hits the limit of parallel active sessions (usually **5** per user)

---

## Alternative for server-side automation (no user login)

If you do not need per-user OAuth sessions, use the **JWT auth flow** instead. JWT credentials can be configured to never expire; you request a new access token with `grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer` whenever needed — no refresh token, and no user login prompt. JWT is intended for server scripts and single service accounts, not multi-user apps where each user signs in individually.

---

**Sources:** [Refresh tokens](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/authentication/refresh-tokens), [Choosing the best auth method](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/authentication/index), [Session keep-alive](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/authentication/session-keep-alive), [Register app — refresh tokens](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/getting-started/register-app), [Authentication errors](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/basics/errors)

results.question: Which RingCentral SDKs have a dedicated WebSocket or real-time communication library beyond the standard REST SDK, and what is each called?
results.ground_truth: Beyond the standard REST SDKs, RingCentral provides: (1) @ringcentral/subscriptions — Node.js npm package for WebSocket push subscriptions (install: npm install @ringcentral/subscriptions); (2) ringcentral-websocket-java — Java WebSocket client library; (3) ringcentral-softphone-ts / ringcentral-softphone-js / ringcentral-softphone-go — SIP/WebRTC softphone SDKs for audio streaming in TypeScript, JavaScript, and Go respectively; (4) ringcentral-web-phone — full WebRTC-based browser phone SDK. The standard REST SDKs (Python, PHP, Ruby, .NET, Java) handle REST calls only; WebSocket/real-time needs a separate library.

raw.answer:
RingCentral splits this into two categories: **WebSocket event subscriptions** (platform notifications) and **real-time voice** (SIP/WebRTC over WebSocket). Only some languages ship those as **separate libraries**; others bundle WebSocket support inside the main REST SDK.

## WebSocket event subscriptions (separate libraries)

These are add-on packages on top of the standard REST SDK:

| REST SDK | Dedicated WebSocket library | Package / repo name |
|----------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| **JavaScript/Node** (`@ringcentral/sdk`) | **RingCentral Subscriptions SDK** | `@ringcentral/subscriptions` (`ringcentral-js/subscriptions`) |
| **TypeScript (Extensible SDK)** (`@rc-ex/core`) | **WebSocket Extension** | `@rc-ex/ws` (`ringcentral-extensible/packages/extensions/ws`) |
| **Java** (`com.ringcentral:ringcentral`) | **RingCentral WebSocket SDK for Java** | `com.ringcentral:ringcentral-websocket` (`ringcentral-websocket-java`) |
| **.NET** (`RingCentral.Net`) | **RingCentral.Net.WebSocket** | NuGet package `RingCentral.Net.WebSocket` |

The Java and .NET REST SDK READMEs explicitly point to these separate WebSocket packages rather than including WebSocket support in the core library.

## Real-time voice communication (separate libraries)

These go beyond REST for live calling (WebRTC/SIP over WebSocket):

| Language | Library | Package / repo name |
|----------|---------|---------------------|
| **JavaScript/TypeScript (browser)** | **RingCentral Web Phone SDK** | `ringcentral-web-phone` |
| **TypeScript/JavaScript (server-side)** | **RingCentral Softphone SDK** (Cloud Phone SDK) | `ringcentral-softphone` (`ringcentral-softphone-ts`; `ringcentral-softphone-js` is deprecated in favor of this) |
| **Go** | **RingCentral Softphone SDK for Go** | `github.com/ringcentral/ringcentral-softphone-go` |

## Built into the REST SDK (not separate)

These REST SDKs include WebSocket subscription support in the main package, so they do **not** have a dedicated sibling library in the monorepo:

- **Python** — `ringcentral` (WebSocket module in the main SDK)
- **Ruby** — `ringcentral_sdk` (WebSocket subscriptions via `WS`)
- **PHP** — `ringcentral/ringcentral-php` (`RingCentral\SDK\WebSocket` namespace)

## No dedicated WebSocket/real-time library in this monorepo

- **Go** REST SDK (`ringcentral-go`) — no WebSocket subscription library
- **Swift** REST SDK (`ringcentral-swift`) — no WebSocket support found
- **Video SDKs** — the monorepo has video **samples** only, not separate open-source Video SDK repos with WebSocket libraries

**Summary:** Four languages have **dedicated WebSocket subscription libraries** (JS/TS via `@ringcentral/subscriptions` and `@rc-ex/ws`, Java, .NET), and three have **dedicated real-time voice libraries** (Web Phone, Softphone TS/JS, Softphone Go). Python, Ruby, and PHP handle WebSocket subscriptions inside their standard REST SDKs.

mintlify.answer:
RingCentral documents **two separate add-on libraries** for WebSocket event notifications, plus several **JavaScript-only real-time libraries** for voice/WebRTC. The other official REST SDKs can use WebSockets, but not via a separately named package.

## Dedicated WebSocket libraries (beyond the REST SDK)

These are **separate packages** installed on top of the standard REST SDK:

| Language / platform | Library name | Package / install |
|---|---|---|
| **JavaScript / Node.js** (also recommended for mobile) | **`@ringcentral/subscriptions`** | `npm install @ringcentral/subscriptions --save` (used with `@ringcentral/sdk`) |
| **.NET / C#** | **`RingCentral.Net.WebSocket`** | Add NuGet package alongside `RingCentral.Net` (e.g. `RingCentral.Net` 6.3.1 + `RingCentral.Net.WebSocket` 2.3.4) |

The [SDKs page](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/sdks) explicitly calls out only Node.js for WebSocket subscriptions:

> For WebSocket subscriptions (Node.js): `npm install @ringcentral/subscriptions`

The [notifications index](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/notifications/index) describes `@ringcentral/subscriptions` as the WebSocket package for **Node.js / mobile**. It handles WebSocket connection management, reconnection, and event subscription without a public server.

For .NET, the [WebSockets quick start](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/notifications/websockets/quick-start) requires **both** `RingCentral.Net` and the dedicated `RingCentral.Net.WebSocket` NuGet package.

---

## Real-time communication libraries (beyond REST)

These are **JavaScript specialized SDKs** for real-time voice/WebRTC, listed under [Specialized SDKs](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/sdks#specialized-sdks):

| Library name | GitHub repo / identifier | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Web Phone SDK** (RingCentral WebPhone Client Library) | [`ringcentral-web-phone`](https://github.com/ringcentral/ringcentral-web-phone) | Browser WebRTC voice client; SIP call controls (wrapper over SIP.js) |
| **Cloud Phone SDK** | [`ringcentral-softphone-ts`](https://ringcentral.github.io/ringcentral-softphone-ts/) | Real-time audio stream interaction (call recording, streaming audio into a call) |
| **Embeddable** | [`ringcentral-embeddable`](https://ringcentral.github.io/ringcentral-embeddable/) | Pre-built embeddable web phone widget (built on the Web Phone SDK) |
| **RingCentral Call JS Library** | [`ringcentral-call-js`](https://github.com/ringcentral/ringcentral-call-js) | Call control across devices; built on Web Phone SDK + Call Control REST API |

The SDKs page also lists `ringcentral-web-phone` as a **“JavaScript WebRTC library”** under REST APIs.

---

## REST SDKs with WebSocket support but no separate library

Python, Ruby, Java, and PHP have [WebSockets quick starts](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/notifications/websockets/quick-start) that use the **standard REST SDK only**:

| Language | Standard REST SDK | WebSocket install |
|---|---|---|
| Python | `ringcentral` | `pip3 install ringcentral python-dotenv` |
| Ruby | `ringcentral-sdk` | `gem install ringcentral-sdk dotenv` |
| Java | `com.ringcentral:ringcentral` | Main Java SDK in `build.gradle` |
| PHP | `ringcentral/ringcentral-php` | `composer require ringcentral/ringcentral-php vlucas/phpdotenv` |

**Go** (`github.com/ringcentral/ringcentral-go`) has no documented dedicated WebSocket or real-time library.

---

## Summary

**Dedicated WebSocket packages beyond the REST SDK:** only **JavaScript/Node.js** (`@ringcentral/subscriptions`) and **.NET** (`RingCentral.Net.WebSocket`).

**Dedicated real-time libraries beyond REST:** JavaScript-only — **Web Phone SDK**, **Cloud Phone SDK**, **Embeddable**, and **RingCentral Call JS Library**.

For manual WebSocket connections (without the add-on libraries), the docs describe connecting to:

`wss://servername.ringcentral.com/ws?access_token=<token>`

after obtaining a token via `POST /restapi/oauth/wstoken`.
